metadata.subject: Physical and Health Education
metadata.description: Physical and Health Education 12 is the capstone PHE course, challenging students to synthesize and apply all previous physical literacy and health knowledge as they prepare for post-secondary life. Students demonstrate leadership in physical activity, design and evaluate advanced personal fitness programs, engage deeply with health advocacy, and develop a personal wellness plan for life after graduation. Complex health topics — including mental health systems, chronic disease prevention, reproductive planning, and global health issues — are explored at a sophisticated level. First Peoples perspectives on intergenerational wellness, land-based healing, and community health leadership are central.
metadata.general_description: The capstone PHE course synthesizing physical literacy, health leadership, advanced wellness planning, and community health advocacy — preparing students to lead healthy, active lives and contribute to community well-being post-graduation.

big_ideas.statement: Daily participation in different types of physical activity influences our physical literacy and personal health and fitness goals.
big_ideas.elaboration: Graduating students are positioned to take full, lifelong ownership of their physical activity lives — understanding the science of movement, the diversity of activity options, and their own preferences deeply enough to sustain active living independently.

big_ideas.statement: Lifelong participation in physical activity has many benefits and is an essential part of a healthy lifestyle.
big_ideas.elaboration: Grade 12 students examine the evidence base for physical activity across the lifespan — including aging, chronic disease prevention, mental health maintenance, and social connection — and develop plans for sustaining activity post-graduation.

big_ideas.statement: Healthy choices influence our physical, emotional, and mental well-being.
big_ideas.elaboration: At graduation, students integrate their health knowledge into a coherent personal wellness philosophy — understanding the complexity of health determinants and developing a realistic, values-based approach to well-being in adult life.

big_ideas.statement: Healthy relationships can help us lead rewarding and fulfilling lives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students at the graduation level reflect on the relationship competencies they have developed and articulate how they will apply these skills in the more complex relational landscape of post-secondary and adult life.

big_ideas.statement: Advocating for the health and well-being of others connects us to our community.
big_ideas.elaboration: Graduating students are equipped to be genuine health advocates — in their post-secondary environments, workplaces, families, and communities — carrying forward the understanding that individual health is connected to collective well-being.

competencies.raw: Develop, refine, and apply fundamental movement skills in various physical activities; develop and apply movement concepts and strategies in different activities; apply methods for monitoring and adjusting exertion levels; demonstrate safety, fair play, and leadership in physical activities; identify preferred types of physical activity.
i_can_statements: I can apply sophisticated movement skills and strategies across a diverse range of physical activities, including lifetime and recreational activities.
I can analyze and teach movement concepts and tactical strategies to others.
I can design and implement a personal training monitoring system that guides safe and effective fitness development.
I can lead and model excellence in safety, fair play, and sportsmanship as a senior student and community member.
I can articulate a detailed, realistic personal physical activity plan for post-graduation life.

competencies.raw: Participate daily in fitness-enhancing physical activity; describe how school, home, and community activities influence health; develop strategies for promoting healthy eating choices; assess factors influencing healthy choices; identify and analyze health message sources and their behavioural influence; identify and apply personal healthy-living goal strategies; reflect on outcomes and assess personal goal strategies.
i_can_statements: I can independently sustain a fitness program aligned with evidence-based guidelines for adult health.
I can synthesize knowledge of health determinants into a coherent understanding of how lifestyle, environment, and society shape wellness.
I can develop a comprehensive, realistic nutrition strategy for post-graduation life, accounting for budget, time, and social context.
I can critically deconstruct wellness culture, health trends, and commercial health messaging using research literacy.
I can create and present a comprehensive personal wellness plan for post-graduation life, integrating all dimensions of health.

competencies.raw: Propose strategies for avoiding and/or responding to unsafe or exploitative situations; propose strategies for responding to discrimination, stereotyping, and bullying; propose strategies for developing and maintaining healthy relationships; create strategies for promoting school and community well-being.
i_can_statements: I can lead others in recognizing and responding to unsafe, coercive, and exploitative situations with confidence and effectiveness.
I can take a leadership role in challenging discrimination, stereotyping, and oppression in my school and community.
I can mentor peers in developing healthy relationship skills and support those experiencing relationship difficulties.
I can design, lead, and evaluate meaningful health promotion initiatives within my school or community.

competencies.raw: Describe and assess strategies for promoting mental well-being; describe and assess strategies for managing mental health and substance use problems; create and assess strategies for managing physical, emotional, and social changes during puberty; explore and describe transition and change impacts on identities.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate and advocate for evidence-based mental well-being strategies at individual, school, and community levels.
I can demonstrate sophisticated mental health literacy — accurately describing disorders, treatments, and support systems — and apply it in peer advocacy.
I can design and evaluate a personal mental wellness plan that addresses the unique stressors of post-graduation transition.
I can articulate how my identity, experiences, and values will shape my well-being and relationships in adult life.

areas.name: Physical Activity Leadership
areas.raw: Movement skill application, lifetime activities, physical activity leadership, and coaching.
topics: Lifetime physical activity: exploring and committing to activities sustainable across adulthood — swimming, cycling, hiking, yoga, team sports
Physical activity leadership: planning, teaching, and evaluating physical activity experiences for others
Coaching and instruction: applying principles of effective feedback, skill progression, and motivation
Inclusive physical activity: designing and adapting activities for diverse abilities and needs
Outdoor and environmental fitness: trail running, outdoor fitness, and land stewardship
First Peoples traditional games and physical practices: cultural significance and contemporary applications

areas.name: Advanced Fitness and Performance
areas.raw: Advanced fitness program design, sport science, monitoring, and long-term athlete development.
topics: Long-term wellness planning: designing a fitness program for the post-graduation transition (university, work, independent living)
Sport science fundamentals: energy systems, muscle physiology, biomechanics, and performance nutrition
Advanced periodization: designing annual training plans for health or sport performance
Strength and conditioning: resistance training program design for safety, effectiveness, and goal alignment
Chronic disease prevention: the role of physical activity in preventing cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and mental illness
Recovery optimization: sleep, nutrition timing, active recovery, and stress management as training variables
Fitness testing and reporting: conducting, interpreting, and communicating fitness assessment results

areas.name: Health Advocacy and Wellness Planning
areas.raw: Post-graduation wellness planning, health equity, global health, and community health leadership.
topics: Personal wellness plan: creating a comprehensive, realistic plan integrating physical, mental, social, and nutritional wellness for post-graduation life
Health equity and social determinants: how income, race, gender, housing, and education shape health outcomes
Global health issues: communicable disease, climate change and health, food security, and universal health care
Health policy and advocacy: understanding BC and Canadian health systems and how to advocate for health equity
Chronic disease management: supporting family and community members living with chronic conditions
Post-secondary health resources: navigating campus health, counselling, wellness centres, and community services
First Peoples health sovereignty: Indigenous approaches to healing, wellness, and health system advocacy

areas.name: Social and Community Health
areas.raw: Relationships, gender and sexuality, community health leadership, and peer advocacy.
topics: Adult relationships: communication in long-term partnerships, cohabitation, and navigating adult social worlds
Sexual and reproductive health: advanced contraception, fertility, pregnancy options, and reproductive rights
Gender-based violence: understanding root causes, impacts, and community prevention strategies
LGBTQ+ health: specific health considerations, affirming care, and advocating for inclusive health environments
Community health leadership: designing and sustaining peer health initiatives and wellness campaigns
Health communication: effectively sharing health information with diverse audiences
Intercultural health: respecting cultural differences in health beliefs and practices

areas.name: Mental Well-being
areas.raw: Post-graduation mental health, wellness systems, resilience, and mental health leadership.
topics: Post-graduation transition: managing the psychological challenges of leaving school — identity shift, independence, and uncertainty
Mental health systems: understanding post-secondary mental health services, community supports, and crisis resources
Advanced mental health literacy: eating disorders, personality disorders, psychosis, and effective support approaches
Addictions and recovery: understanding the neuroscience of addiction and evidence-based recovery pathways
Mindfulness and mind-body practices: yoga, meditation, breathing techniques, and their evidence base
Resilience and post-traumatic growth: understanding how people recover from adversity and grow through challenge
Mental health leadership: advocating for mental health support, leading anti-stigma efforts, and supporting peers in crisis
